quote: The same head-final logic ramifies down into the noun phrase — modifiers, relative clauses, and property verbs precede the head noun — and upward into clause-level relations — subordinate clauses precede main clauses, and markers that relate an NP to the predicate follow the NP as postpositions.
issue: The citation <Ref k="nakagawa2024" p="363" /> is attached to the claim about verb-finality, but the sentence it is attached to discusses NP and clause-level relations, which are not directly supported by that specific page reference.
suggestion: Move the citation to the end of the paragraph or verify if page 363 covers these points.

quote: Bugaeva gives the canonical summary: "the basic constituent order in Ainu is SV/AOV. Modifiers are prepositive, and subordinate clauses come before main clauses. All particles are postpositional. Topical NPs tend to come first" <Ref k="bugaeva2012" p="472" />.
issue: The citation <Ref k="bugaeva2012" p="472" /> is placed after the quote, but the quote itself contains multiple claims about constituent order, modifiers, subordinate clauses, particles, and topical NPs. It's unclear if page 472 supports all these points comprehensively.
suggestion: Verify if page 472 of Bugaeva (2012) supports all aspects of the quoted summary, or break down the quote and cite accordingly.

quote: The surface resemblance to Japanese — both verb-final, both postpositional — therefore conceals a fundamental typological difference: Japanese is dependent-marking, with case particles on the NP encoding grammatical relations; Ainu is head-marking, with person markers on the verb doing that work <Ref k="sato2025a" p="359–360" />; <Ref k="bugaeva2012" p="466" />.
issue: The citation <Ref k="sato2025a" p="359–360" /> and <Ref k="bugaeva2012" p="466" /> are placed at the end of a sentence that makes a strong typological claim contrasting Japanese and Ainu. It's possible these pages only support parts of the claim, or that a more comprehensive citation is needed.
suggestion: Ensure that the cited pages fully support the contrast between Japanese and Ainu regarding dependent-marking vs. head-marking.

quote: Refsing's Shizunai data present a third position <Ref k="refsing1986" paren />, §2.4, reporting Chiri (1936: 167): a default SOV order when both subject and object are full NPs, with systematic deviation to O–S–V when the subject is a prefixal person marker (the object NP then precedes the prefix-bearing verb). Three-place clauses show "flexible" ordering, with IO before DO as the most frequent pattern when the subject is a prefix (§13.4.3). These regularities match Nakagawa's Chitose data as statistical tendencies, not as categorical rules ‹contested›.

suggestion: Add a citation for Nakagawa's data that supports the comparison.

quote: The head noun toma 'mat' and the numeral-noun sinep 'one' are separated by the focus particle ka 'also/even'; yet isam 'not exist' is a one-place intransitive, so toma and sinep must together fill the single S slot. Nakagawa analyses [[toma] ka [sinep] ka] as a discontinuous NP — a classic indicator of pragmatically free constituent structure. Community discussion notes that the N + adverbial-numeral + V order (this N ka Num V pattern) appears frequently in Ainu oral texts, perhaps more so than comparable structures in Japanese (nukopoli, aynu-corpora Discord 2024-12-12, proposed ‹contested›).
issue: The note mentions 'adverbial-numeral' for 'sinep', but the gloss in the example is 'one', which is a numeral. The term 'adverbial-numeral' might be a typo or a specific technical term that needs clarification.
suggestion: Clarify the term 'adverbial-numeral' or use a more standard term like 'numeral' if appropriate.
